wrapper.intro_html:
How to Use This Guide

Welcome. This guide teaches Security & Privacy Engineering — the discipline of building software that protects data and resists attackers, while respecting the people whose information it holds. It is written for a specific reader: a working software engineer who can already ship features, but who has never studied security as its own field. If you can write an API endpoint but freeze when someone says "is this endpoint vulnerable to IDOR?" or "where's our data-retention policy?" — this guide is for you.

You do not need a security background to start. Every technical term is defined in plain words the first time it appears. We begin with the absolute basics (what a "threat" even is) and build steadily toward advanced material (threat modeling, DevSecOps, AI/LLM attacks, incident response). Think of it as going from "I write code" to "I write code an attacker can't easily turn against me — and a regulator can't fine me for."

How the callout boxes work

Throughout the guide you'll see colored boxes. Each has one job, so you can skim or study depending on your energy:

Key takeaway
The single most important idea of a section. If you read nothing else, read these. Every section ends with one as a summary.
Best practice
A concrete, do-this-in-real-life recommendation you can apply at work tomorrow.
Common mistake
A trap engineers fall into repeatedly. Learning the mistake is often faster than learning the rule.
Analogy
An everyday comparison (locks, passports, sealed envelopes) that makes an abstract idea click.
Example
A real-world scenario, incident, or short walkthrough showing the concept in action.

Suggested reading order

If you're new to the field, read it front to back. The sections are deliberately ordered so each one assumes only what came before. Foundations (1–3) make everything else readable; skipping them is the most common reason later chapters feel confusing.

  • Short on time? Read sections 1, 2, 4, and 5 — that's the practical core most developers need first.
  • Already know the basics? Use the section list below as a menu and jump to what you need (e.g., 12 for AI security, 11 for compliance).
  • Studying for a role or interview? Pair each chapter's "Common mistakes" and "Best practices" lists — they map closely to what's tested in practice.

The 14 sections — your journey

  1. Why Security & Privacy Engineering Matters — the business and human stakes; why this is now everyone's job.
  2. Core Security Foundations — the CIA triad, threats vs. vulnerabilities vs. risk, defense in depth, least privilege.
  3. Cryptography Made Simple — encryption, hashing, keys, and TLS explained without the math.
  4. Authentication & Authorization — proving who you are vs. what you're allowed to do; passwords, MFA, OAuth, tokens.
  5. Application & Web Security — the OWASP Top 10 in practice: injection, XSS, CSRF, broken access control.
  6. Network, Cloud & Infrastructure Security — firewalls, zero trust, secrets, and securing your cloud accounts.
  7. Threat Modeling & Risk Management — thinking like an attacker before you ship; STRIDE and prioritizing risk.
  8. Security Testing & Auditing — SAST, DAST, dependency scanning, and penetration testing.
  9. Secure SDLC & DevSecOps — baking security into every stage of the development pipeline.
  10. Privacy Engineering Fundamentals — data minimization, anonymization, and privacy by design.
  11. Privacy Laws & Compliance — GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, and what they actually require of engineers.
  12. AI & LLM Security and Privacy — prompt injection, data leakage, and securing AI features (the 2025–2026 frontier).
  13. Detection, Monitoring & Incident Response — spotting attacks, logging, and what to do when something breaks.
  14. Becoming a Security & Privacy Engineer — skills, certifications, and a career roadmap.
Best practice: Keep a running "apply-at-work" list as you read. Every time a Best practice or Common mistake box matches something in your own codebase, jot it down. By the end you'll have a real, prioritized security to-do list — not just notes.
Key takeaway: This guide takes you from security beginner to capable practitioner in 14 ordered steps. Read front to back if you're new, lean on the callout boxes to control your depth, and treat each chapter as something to apply — not just to memorize. Security is learned by doing, and you can start applying it from section 1.
wrapper.glossary_html:
Glossary of Terms

This glossary collects the most important terms used across security and privacy engineering. Each entry is written in plain English so you can use it as a quick reference while reading the rest of the guide. Terms are grouped roughly in alphabetical order. Where a concept has a common abbreviation, the abbreviation is listed first.

AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)
The three jobs of access control: prove who you are (authentication), decide what you may do (authorization), and record what you did (accounting/auditing).
ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)
An access model that grants permission based on attributes (facts) about the user, the resource, and the context, e.g. "managers in the EU office may view EU orders during business hours." More flexible than fixed roles.
Anonymization
Permanently stripping data so a person can no longer be identified, even by combining it with other data. True anonymization is hard; once done it usually falls outside privacy laws.
Asymmetric cryptography (public-key crypto)
Encryption that uses a key pair: a public key anyone can hold and a private key kept secret. Data encrypted with one key is decrypted with the other. Powers TLS, digital signatures, and SSH. Examples: RSA, ECC.
CA (Certificate Authority)
A trusted organization that issues digital certificates vouching that a public key really belongs to a given website. Browsers ship with a list of CAs they trust (e.g. Let's Encrypt, DigiCert).
CCPA / CPRA
California's consumer privacy laws giving residents rights to know, delete, correct, and opt out of the "sale" or sharing of their personal data. The CPRA (2023) strengthened the original CCPA.
CIA Triad
The three goals of security: Confidentiality (only authorized people see data), Integrity (data is not tampered with), Availability (systems work when needed). Almost every control supports one of these.
Controller vs. Processor
GDPR roles. The controller decides why and how personal data is processed (e.g. an online store). The processor handles data on the controller's behalf (e.g. a cloud email service). Each has different legal duties.
CSP (Content Security Policy)
A browser security rule, sent as an HTTP header, that tells the browser which sources of scripts, styles, and images are allowed. A strong CSP is a key defense against XSS.
CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)
An attack that tricks a logged-in user's browser into sending an unwanted request (like changing their email) to a site they trust. Defended with anti-CSRF tokens and the SameSite cookie attribute.
CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)
A public catalog that gives each known security flaw a unique ID (e.g. CVE-2021-44228 for Log4Shell), so everyone refers to the same bug.
CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)
A 0-10 score rating how severe a vulnerability is based on how easy it is to exploit and how much damage it does. Version 4.0 is the current standard.
DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing)
Security testing that probes a running application from the outside, like an attacker would, sending malicious inputs to find flaws. Complements SAST.
Defense in Depth
Using many overlapping layers of security so that if one fails, others still protect you. Like a castle with a moat, walls, and guards.
Differential Privacy
A mathematical technique that adds carefully calibrated random noise to data or query results so individuals cannot be singled out, while overall statistics stay useful. Used by Apple and the US Census.
DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment)
A formal risk review, required by GDPR for high-risk processing, that documents what personal data is used, the risks to people, and how those risks are reduced.
DSAR (Data Subject Access Request)
A request by an individual to see, correct, or delete the personal data a company holds about them. Privacy laws set deadlines for responding.
EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response)
Software on laptops and servers that watches for malicious behavior, alerts security teams, and can isolate an infected machine. Examples: CrowdStrike, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint.
EPSS (Exploit Prediction Scoring System)
A score estimating the probability that a vulnerability will be exploited in the wild in the next 30 days. Helps teams patch the bugs that actually matter, not just the high-CVSS ones.
EU AI Act
The European Union's 2024 law regulating artificial intelligence by risk level. It bans some uses outright, imposes strict rules on "high-risk" AI, and adds transparency duties for general-purpose models. Phasing in through 2026-2027.
Excessive Agency
An LLM (large language model) risk where an AI system is given too much power, autonomy, or permissions, so a manipulated model can take harmful real-world actions (e.g. deleting files or sending money). Listed in the OWASP Top 10 for LLMs.
Exploit
A specific piece of code or technique that takes advantage of a vulnerability to cause harm. The vulnerability is the open window; the exploit is climbing through it.
GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)
The EU's comprehensive privacy law (2018). Sets strict rules for handling personal data, gives individuals strong rights, and allows fines up to 4% of global revenue.
Hashing
A one-way function that turns any input into a fixed-length fingerprint. You cannot reverse it to get the original. Used to store passwords (with a salt) and verify file integrity. Examples: SHA-256, bcrypt, Argon2.
HIPAA
A US law protecting the privacy and security of health information held by healthcare providers, insurers, and their vendors.
HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)
An HTTP header telling browsers to only ever connect to a site over encrypted HTTPS, blocking downgrade attacks.
IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)
A flaw where changing an ID in a URL or request (e.g. /invoice/123 to /invoice/124) lets you see someone else's data because the server never checks ownership. A leading cause of data leaks.
Incident Response (IR)
The organized process of detecting, containing, eradicating, and recovering from a security breach, then learning from it. Often summarized as prepare, identify, contain, eradicate, recover, review.
JWT (JSON Web Token)
A compact, signed token carrying user claims (like user ID and roles) that a server can verify without a database lookup. Common in APIs. Must be validated carefully to avoid forgery.
k-Anonymity
A privacy property where each record is indistinguishable from at least k-1 others on identifying fields, so no single person stands out in a released dataset.
KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities)
A catalog maintained by the US agency CISA listing vulnerabilities confirmed to be actively exploited. If a bug is on the KEV list, patch it now.
Least Privilege
Give every user, service, and process only the minimum access it needs to do its job, and no more. Limits the damage from a compromised account.
LINDDUN
A threat-modeling framework focused on privacy threats: Linking, Identifying, Non-repudiation, Detecting, Data disclosure, Unawareness, Non-compliance. The privacy counterpart to STRIDE.
MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication)
Requiring two or more proofs of identity from different categories: something you know (password), something you have (phone/token), something you are (fingerprint). Greatly reduces account takeover.
MITRE ATT&CK
A free, widely used knowledge base of real attacker tactics and techniques, organized by stage of an attack. Used to plan defenses and map detections.
Model Supply Chain
All the components an AI system depends on: training data, pre-trained models, libraries, and plugins. A poisoned dataset or backdoored model downloaded from a public hub is a supply-chain attack on AI.
OAuth 2.0
A standard for delegated authorization: it lets an app access part of your account on another service ("Sign in with Google" granting calendar access) without sharing your password. It is about access, not identity.
OIDC (OpenID Connect)
An identity layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 that adds proof of who the user is (via an ID token), enabling true single sign-on.
Passkey / WebAuthn
A modern, phishing-resistant login that replaces passwords with a cryptographic key pair stored on your device and unlocked by your fingerprint, face, or PIN. WebAuthn is the underlying web standard.
Pentest (Penetration Test)
An authorized simulated attack by skilled testers to find and prove real, exploitable weaknesses before criminals do.
PETs (Privacy-Enhancing Technologies)
A family of techniques that let you use data while protecting privacy, including differential privacy, homomorphic encryption (computing on encrypted data), secure multi-party computation, and federated learning.
PCI DSS
The Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard: mandatory security rules for any business that stores, processes, or transmits credit card data. Version 4.0.1 is current.
PII (Personally Identifiable Information)
Any data that can identify a person directly or indirectly, e.g. name, email, IP address, device ID. The core thing privacy laws protect.
PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)
The system of certificate authorities, certificates, and rules that lets parties on the internet trust each other's public keys. The trust backbone behind HTTPS.
Prompt Injection
An attack on AI systems where malicious instructions are hidden in user input or fetched content, tricking the model into ignoring its rules or leaking data. The top LLM security risk; "indirect" injection comes from data the model reads.
Pseudonymization
Replacing identifying fields with a reversible token or key (e.g. swapping a name for "User-7842"). Reduces risk but, unlike anonymization, can be reversed, so the data is still personal data under GDPR.
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
An AI design where the model fetches relevant documents from a knowledge base and uses them to answer, improving accuracy. It also opens new risks like indirect prompt injection via poisoned documents.
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
Assigning permissions to named roles (admin, editor, viewer) and then giving users roles. Simple and common; less flexible than ABAC.
Red / Blue / Purple Team
Red teams attack, blue teams defend, and purple teaming is the two working together so defenders learn directly from attacker techniques.
Risk
The combination of how likely a threat is to exploit a vulnerability and how bad the impact would be. Risk management is about reducing this to an acceptable level, not eliminating it.
SAML
An older XML-based standard for single sign-on, still widely used in enterprise and corporate apps. OIDC is the modern alternative.
Salt
A unique random value added to each password before hashing so that identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed "rainbow table" attacks.
SAST (Static Application Security Testing)
Scanning source code without running it to find security bugs early, often inside the developer's editor or CI pipeline.
SBOM (Software Bill of Materials)
A complete inventory of all the components and libraries inside a piece of software, so you can quickly tell whether you're affected when a new vulnerability appears.
SCA (Software Composition Analysis)
Tooling that scans your third-party and open-source dependencies for known vulnerabilities and license issues.
SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)
A system that collects logs from across the organization, correlates them, and raises alerts on suspicious patterns. The control room for a security operations team.
SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts)
A framework (pronounced "salsa") of graded requirements for securing how software is built and delivered, to prevent tampering in the build pipeline.
SOC (Security Operations Center)
The team (and often the room) that monitors, detects, and responds to security threats around the clock.
SQLi (SQL Injection)
An attack that inserts malicious database commands through user input, letting attackers read or destroy data. Prevented with parameterized queries. Still one of the oldest and most damaging web flaws.
SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)
Tricking a server into making requests to addresses the attacker chooses, often to reach internal systems or cloud metadata services. A major cloud-era risk.
STRIDE
A threat-modeling framework listing six threat types: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege.
Symmetric cryptography
Encryption where the same secret key both locks and unlocks the data. Fast and used for bulk encryption. Example: AES. The challenge is sharing the key securely.
Threat
Anything that could harm your systems or data: a hacker, malware, an insider, or even a flood. Distinct from vulnerability and risk.
Threat Modeling
A structured exercise, done early in design, to ask "what can go wrong here?" and decide which defenses to build. Frameworks include STRIDE and LINDDUN.
TLS (Transport Layer Security)
The protocol that encrypts data in transit on the web (the "S" in HTTPS), protecting it from eavesdropping and tampering. TLS 1.3 is the current secure version; SSL is its obsolete predecessor.
Vulnerability
A weakness or flaw in software, configuration, or process that a threat could exploit. The open window an attacker climbs through.
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)
Injecting malicious JavaScript into a web page so it runs in other users' browsers, stealing sessions or data. Prevented by output encoding and a strong CSP.
Zero Trust
A security model that assumes no user or device is trusted by default, even inside the network. Every request must be verified ("never trust, always verify"), with strong identity and least privilege at its core.
Key takeaway: Security and privacy share a small vocabulary that recurs everywhere. Get comfortable with the building blocks, threat/vulnerability/risk, the CIA triad, least privilege, and defense in depth, and the more specialized terms (STRIDE, Zero Trust, prompt injection, GDPR roles) will slot neatly on top.
wrapper.faq_html:
Frequently Asked Questions

By now you have read through the whole guide. This closing section answers the honest, practical questions that beginners actually ask, the ones that do not fit neatly into a single chapter. The answers here are opinionated but grounded in how the field really works in 2025-2026. Read them as advice from someone who has watched many engineers move into security and privacy work.

What is the difference between security and privacy, really?

Security is about protecting data and systems from people who should not have access. Privacy is about controlling what happens to data even among people who do have legitimate access. A simple way to remember it:

  • Security asks: "Can an attacker steal or break this?" It is mostly about confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the CIA triad).
  • Privacy asks: "Should we even collect this, and are we using it the way the person expects?" It is about consent, purpose, minimization, and rights.

You can have perfect security and terrible privacy. A company can encrypt everything flawlessly and still secretly sell your location history to advertisers. That is a privacy failure with no security failure. The two overlap (you cannot have privacy without security) but they are not the same discipline.

Is it easier to break into security or into privacy engineering?

Security has more entry-level roles, more learning resources, more job postings, and a clearer skills ladder, so the front door is wider. Privacy engineering is a younger, smaller field with fewer dedicated junior roles, but it is growing fast and has far less competition. The honest answer for most software engineers: security is the easier first move because your coding background transfers directly (application security, secure SDLC, cloud security). Privacy engineering rewards people who like the intersection of law, data architecture, and ethics, and it is a great specialization once you have a security or data-engineering base. Many people enter through security and drift toward privacy later.

Do I need to be good at math to do cryptography?

To use cryptography correctly: no. To invent cryptography: yes, a lot. The vast majority of security engineers never design an algorithm. Your job is to pick the right vetted library and use it correctly, choosing AES-GCM over a homemade cipher, using a real key-derivation function for passwords, never rolling your own. That requires understanding concepts (what a hash is, why nonces must not repeat, what forward secrecy means) far more than it requires number theory.

Common mistake: Avoiding security because you "are not a math person." The single most important crypto skill, knowing not to build your own, requires zero math.

Will AI automate security jobs away?

No, but it will reshape them. AI is excellent at the high-volume, repetitive parts: triaging alerts, summarizing logs, drafting detection rules, scanning code for known patterns. That removes grunt work, not judgment. At the same time, AI creates a whole new attack surface (prompt injection, model theft, data leakage, autonomous attacker tooling) that needs human defenders. The pattern across the industry is the same as with every prior automation wave: the floor of tedious work rises, and demand shifts toward people who can reason about systems, threat-model novel attacks, and supervise AI tools. Engineers who use AI will replace engineers who refuse to, but the role is not going away.

How is AI/LLM security different from "normal" security?

Traditional security assumes a clear boundary between code (trusted instructions) and data (untrusted input). Large language models erase that boundary: to an LLM, instructions and data are the same stream of text. That is why prompt injection (hiding malicious instructions inside content the model reads, like a web page or an email) is the defining new vulnerability and has no clean fix the way SQL injection does with parameterized queries.

  • You cannot fully "sanitize" natural language the way you escape SQL.
  • Defenses are layered and probabilistic (least privilege for tools, human approval for risky actions, output filtering), not deterministic.
  • New concerns appear: training-data leakage, model output that fabricates facts, and AI agents taking real actions with real consequences.

If you already know application security, you are well positioned, because most LLM defenses are old principles (least privilege, input distrust, output validation) applied to a new, fuzzier component.

What is the single most common vulnerability I should worry about?

Two answers, depending on how you count. By raw frequency of breaches, broken access control, code that fails to check whether this user is allowed to do this action, has been the number-one category on the OWASP Top 10 and is the root of most data leaks (the classic "change the ID in the URL and see someone else's order"). By number of catastrophic incidents, injection (SQL injection, command injection) and stolen/weak credentials stay near the top. If you fix only one class of bug in your career, make it access control: always verify ownership and permission on every request, server-side.

Example: In a multi-tenant app, an endpoint loads an order by ID but never checks the order belongs to the logged-in customer's tenant. Anyone can read everyone's orders by guessing IDs. That is broken access control, the most common real-world breach, and it is usually a one-line fix once found.

Do I need certifications to get hired?

Certifications help most when you have no security job yet, because they signal commitment and give recruiters a keyword to filter on. They matter less once you have real experience. Rough guidance:

  • Foundational: CompTIA Security+ is the common starting cert, broad and beginner-friendly.
  • Offensive: OSCP (hands-on penetration testing) is highly respected because it is a grueling practical exam, not multiple choice.
  • Management/breadth: CISSP is the well-known senior cert, but it requires years of experience and is aimed at leadership, not beginners.
  • Cloud: the cloud providers' security specialty certs (AWS, Azure, GCP) are valuable because so much work is now cloud-based.
  • Privacy: the IAPP certifications (CIPP for law, CIPM for management, CIPT for technologists) are the recognized credentials in privacy.

A portfolio of real work (a home lab, write-ups, CTF results, an open-source contribution) often beats a stack of certs for engineering roles.

Is penetration testing the only way into security?

No, and this is the biggest myth. Pentesting (paid, authorized hacking to find flaws) is glamorous but is a small slice of the field. There are far more jobs in defensive and building roles:

  • Application/product security (reviewing code, building secure features) - best fit for software engineers.
  • Cloud and infrastructure security.
  • Detection and incident response (the SOC, blue team).
  • Security engineering/DevSecOps (building the guardrails and pipelines).
  • Governance, risk, and compliance, and privacy engineering.

For most readers of this guide, application or product security is the most natural and in-demand path, not pentesting.

How do small teams or startups do security with no budget?

Most security wins are free or cheap and come from discipline, not products. A realistic no-budget starting checklist:

  • Turn on multi-factor authentication everywhere, especially admin and cloud accounts.
  • Use a password manager for the whole team.
  • Enable the free security scanners built into GitHub/GitLab (dependency alerts, secret scanning, CodeQL).
  • Patch dependencies regularly; most breaches use known, already-fixed bugs.
  • Apply least privilege in your cloud (no one uses the root/admin account daily).
  • Centralize logs and set a few high-signal alerts.
  • Write a one-page incident plan before you need it.
Best practice: Pick free, high-leverage controls first. MFA plus dependency patching plus least privilege prevents a large share of real-world breaches and costs nothing but time.

Where do I start practicing legally?

Never test systems you do not own or lack written permission for, that is a crime. Use these legal, purpose-built playgrounds:

  • Deliberately vulnerable apps you run yourself: OWASP Juice Shop, DVWA, WebGoat.
  • Guided learning platforms: TryHackMe (beginner-friendly), Hack The Box, PortSwigger Web Security Academy (free and excellent for web bugs).
  • Capture The Flag (CTF) competitions for gamified practice.
  • Bug bounty programs once you have skills, where companies explicitly invite testing within stated rules (HackerOne, Bugcrowd).

I am a developer. What is the fastest path to becoming useful in security?

Lean on what you already have. Your coding ability is a head start most security beginners lack. Concretely: learn the OWASP Top 10 deeply and find each bug class in your own codebase; complete the free PortSwigger Web Security Academy; start threat-modeling features you build ("what could go wrong here?"); volunteer to be your team's security reviewer. Within months you can become the person who catches access-control and injection bugs in code review, which is genuinely valuable and a direct on-ramp to an application-security role.

How do I keep up when the field changes so fast?

You do not memorize everything; you build durable mental models and follow a few good sources. The principles (least privilege, defense in depth, distrust input, minimize data) barely change, while the tools and attacks change constantly. Stay current by reading a small number of trusted feeds, following major incident write-ups (real breaches teach more than any course), and revisiting the OWASP projects and your cloud provider's security guidance, which are updated regularly. Depth in fundamentals plus a habit of reading post-incident reports beats chasing every new tool.

Key takeaway: Security and privacy are far more accessible to working engineers than they look from the outside. You do not need heavy math, certifications, or a pentesting badge to start, you need solid fundamentals (especially access control and input distrust), free hands-on practice in legal labs, and the habit of asking "what could go wrong?" of everything you build. AI changes the tools, not the principles. Start where your existing skills give you leverage, and grow from there.
wrapper.cheatsheet_html:
Revision Cheat Sheet

This is your night-before review. It compresses the whole guide into scannable tables and tight lists. Skim it top to bottom; if any line surprises you, jump back to the full section it summarizes. Every acronym is spelled out the first time so you never have to guess.

The Two Cores: CIA + AAA

CIA triad is the goal of security; AAA is how you enforce access.

LetterMeansPlain EnglishBroken when…
CConfidentialityOnly allowed people see the dataData leak / eavesdropping
IIntegrityData is not secretly changedTampering / corrupted records
AAvailabilityThe system is up when neededDDoS / ransomware outage
AAuthenticationProving who you areStolen credentials
AAuthorizationDeciding what you may doPrivilege escalation
AAccounting (Audit)Logging who did what, whenNo forensic trail

Security Design Principles (memorize)

  • Least privilege — give the minimum access needed, nothing more.
  • Defense in depth — many layers; one failing doesn't sink you.
  • Fail securely / fail closed — errors deny access, never grant it.
  • Zero trust — never trust by network location; verify every request.
  • Secure by default — safest config ships out of the box.
  • Complete mediation — check authorization on every access, not once.
  • Economy of mechanism (KISS) — simple systems have fewer bugs.
  • Open design — security must not rely on secret algorithms (no security by obscurity).
  • Separation of duties — no single person can complete a sensitive action alone.
  • Minimize attack surface — fewer ports, endpoints, and features = fewer doors.
Analogy: Defense in depth is a castle: moat, wall, gate, guards, locked vault. An attacker who jumps the moat still faces the wall. One layer is never enough.

Crypto Quick-Pick (when to use what)

GoalUseAvoid
Encrypt data at rest / in bulkAES-256-GCM (symmetric, authenticated)AES-ECB, DES, RC4
Key exchange / encrypt for someone elseRSA-OAEP or ECDH (asymmetric)RSA-PKCS#1v1.5, raw RSA
Sign / verify (integrity + origin)Ed25519 or ECDSA (P-256)MD5/SHA-1 signatures
Hash data (fingerprint)SHA-256 / SHA-3MD5, SHA-1 (broken)
Store passwordsArgon2id (or bcrypt/scrypt)SHA-256, plain hash, no salt
Verify message authenticity with shared keyHMAC-SHA-256Home-rolled MAC
Transport securityTLS 1.3SSL, TLS 1.0/1.1
Future-proofing (2025-26)Post-quantum: ML-KEM (Kyber), ML-DSA (Dilithium)Ignoring "harvest-now-decrypt-later"
Common mistake: Writing your own crypto, reusing a nonce/IV, or using a fast hash (SHA-256) for passwords. Use vetted libraries; passwords need a slow, salted hash like Argon2id.

Auth Do / Don't

DoDon't
Hash passwords with Argon2id + per-user saltStore or log plaintext passwords
Enforce MFA (multi-factor authentication), prefer phishing-resistant passkeys/FIDO2Rely on SMS OTP as the only second factor
Use short-lived access tokens + rotating refresh tokensPut secrets/roles in a JWT the client can edit and trust it blindly
Check authorization server-side on every requestHide a button and call it access control
Use OAuth2/OIDC with PKCE for delegated authRoll your own session/SSO protocol
Bind sessions to secure, HttpOnly, SameSite cookiesPass session IDs in URLs

Authorization models: RBAC (Role-Based) = access by role; ABAC (Attribute-Based) = access by attributes/context; ReBAC (Relationship-Based, e.g. Google Zanzibar) = access by relationships ("owner of doc X").

OWASP Top 10 (2021) — one-liners

  1. A01 Broken Access Control — users do things they shouldn't (IDOR, privilege escalation).
  2. A02 Cryptographic Failures — weak/missing encryption exposes data.
  3. A03 Injection — untrusted input runs as code (SQLi, command injection, XSS).
  4. A04 Insecure Design — flaw is in the design, not a bug.
  5. A05 Security Misconfiguration — defaults, open buckets, verbose errors.
  6. A06 Vulnerable & Outdated Components — old libraries with known CVEs.
  7. A07 Identification & Auth Failures — weak login, session, MFA.
  8. A08 Software & Data Integrity Failures — untrusted updates/CI (e.g. SolarWinds-style).
  9. A09 Logging & Monitoring Failures — breaches go unseen.
  10. A10 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) — server is tricked into fetching internal URLs.

OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) — one-liners

  1. LLM01 Prompt Injection — malicious input hijacks the model's instructions.
  2. LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure — model leaks PII/secrets.
  3. LLM03 Supply Chain — poisoned models, datasets, or plugins.
  4. LLM04 Data & Model Poisoning — tainted training data corrupts behavior.
  5. LLM05 Improper Output Handling — trusting model output (leads to XSS/RCE).
  6. LLM06 Excessive Agency — agent has too much permission/autonomy.
  7. LLM07 System Prompt Leakage — hidden instructions/secrets exposed.
  8. LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses — RAG store poisoning/leakage.
  9. LLM09 Misinformation — confident hallucinations cause harm.
  10. LLM10 Unbounded Consumption — cost/DoS via runaway queries.
Best practice: Treat all LLM input and output as untrusted. Validate output before it touches a shell, SQL query, browser, or another tool. Give agents least-privilege tools.

STRIDE Threat Model

ThreatViolatesDefense
SpoofingAuthenticationStrong auth, MFA
TamperingIntegrityHashing, signatures, validation
RepudiationNon-repudiationAudit logs, signed records
Information disclosureConfidentialityEncryption, access control
Denial of serviceAvailabilityRate limits, autoscale, CDN
Elevation of privilegeAuthorizationLeast privilege, input validation
 
THREAT MODEL FLOW
  1 Diagram system  -> 2 Find trust boundaries
       |                      |
       v                      v
  4 Rank risk  <-  3 Apply STRIDE per element
       |
       v
  5 Mitigate + re-check (repeat each release)

Security Testing Tools

TypeFull nameWhat it doesWhen
SASTStatic App Security TestingScans source code, no runIn IDE / CI commit
DASTDynamic App Security TestingAttacks the running appStaging / pre-prod
IASTInteractive ASTAgent inside running appQA test runs
SCASoftware Composition AnalysisFinds vulnerable dependenciesEvery build
Secrets scan—Finds leaked keys/tokensPre-commit / CI
PentestPenetration testHumans attack like real adversariesPeriodic / pre-launch
Fuzzing—Floods random input to find crashesCI / continuous

Secure SDLC / DevSecOps Checklist

  • Threat model during design (shift left — fix early, it's cheaper).
  • Security requirements + abuse cases written down.
  • SAST + secrets scan on every commit; SCA on every build.
  • Code review with security checklist; no secrets in git.
  • Secrets in a vault (not env files in repo); rotate regularly.
  • Signed builds + SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) for supply-chain trust.
  • DAST on staging; pentest before major launch.
  • Least-privilege IAM and infrastructure-as-code scanning.
  • Monitoring, alerting, and a tested incident-response plan in production.

Privacy Principles + Key Laws

Core privacy principles: data minimization (collect only what you need), purpose limitation (use it only for the stated reason), consent, transparency, storage limitation (delete when done), privacy by design & by default, and data-subject rights (access, deletion, portability).

PII vs PHI
PII = Personally Identifiable Information (name, email). PHI = Protected Health Information (medical data under HIPAA).
Anonymization vs Pseudonymization
Anonymized = cannot be re-linked (out of scope of most laws). Pseudonymized = key removed but reversible (still personal data).
LawWho/whereOne-liner
GDPREU residentsConsent + rights + breach notice in 72h; fines up to 4% global revenue.
CCPA/CPRACaliforniaRight to know, delete, and opt out of sale of personal info.
HIPAAUS health dataSafeguards for PHI; covered entities + business associates.
PCI DSSCard data (global)Standard for storing/processing payment cards (never store CVV).
EU AI ActEU (2024-26 rollout)Risk tiers; bans some AI uses; rules for high-risk + GPAI models.

Incident Response — PICERL (SANS 6 steps)

  1. Preparation — plan, tools, contacts, runbooks ready before anything happens.
  2. Identification — detect and confirm a real incident.
  3. Containment — stop the spread (isolate hosts, revoke tokens).
  4. Eradication — remove the cause (malware, backdoor, bad creds).
  5. Recovery — restore clean systems and verify they're healthy.
  6. Lessons learned — blameless post-mortem; fix root cause.
Example: Detection layers map to MITRE ATT&CK (a public catalog of attacker techniques). SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) collects logs; EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) watches devices; SOAR automates the response playbook.

Career Quick Map

  • AppSec / Product Security — secure code, threat modeling (your engineering background fits best here).
  • Blue team — defense, detection, IR. Red team — offense, pentest.
  • Cloud/Infra security, GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance), Privacy engineering.
  • Useful certs: Security+ (entry), OSCP (offense), CISSP (broad/senior), CIPP/CIPT (privacy).

Golden Rules

  • Never trust input — validate, sanitize, encode at the boundary.
  • Never roll your own crypto or auth.
  • Encrypt in transit (TLS 1.3) and at rest (AES-256).
  • Least privilege everywhere — humans, services, tokens, AI agents.
  • Patch dependencies; track an SBOM.
  • Log security events; you can't respond to what you can't see.
  • Collect the least data; delete what you don't need.
  • Assume breach — design so one failure isn't game over.
  • Shift left — the cheapest bug to fix is the one caught in design.
  • Treat every LLM input and output as hostile.
Key takeaway: Security and privacy reduce to a handful of repeating ideas: protect Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability; enforce them with strong auth + least-privilege authorization + audit logs; validate all input and output; use vetted crypto; minimize and protect data; test continuously through the SDLC; and assume a breach will happen so you can detect, contain, and recover fast. Master these patterns and any specific technology, law, or attack becomes just another instance to slot into the framework.

sections.title: Why Security & Privacy Engineering Matters
sections.html:
1. Why Security & Privacy Engineering Matters

If you build software, you already shape whether people get robbed, surveilled, or harmed — you just may not realize it yet. Every line of code either opens a door or locks one. This guide teaches you to do it on purpose. Before we get into the how, this section makes the case for why: that security and privacy engineering are two distinct, high-leverage crafts; that demand for them is durable and growing (not about to be automated away); that the attackers are real, organized, and now armed with AI; that regulators worldwide are turning these concerns into legal liability; and that the money at stake is enormous. By the end you'll understand why a working engineer who masters this discipline becomes more valuable as automation advances, not less.

1.1 Two related-but-distinct disciplines

People use "security" and "privacy" as if they were the same thing. They are not. Confusing them is the single most common beginner mistake, so let's separate them clearly.

Security engineering
The craft of protecting systems and data from unauthorized access, tampering, and disruption. The classic mental model is the CIA triad — three properties you are trying to preserve:
  • Confidentiality — only the right people can read the data. (Keep secrets secret.)
  • Integrity — data and systems are not altered by the wrong people or by accident. (You can trust what you see.)
  • Availability — the system is up and usable when needed. (No one can shut you down.)
Security's core question: "Can the wrong person get in, change things, or take us offline?"
Privacy engineering
The craft of building systems that responsibly manage what happens to people's personal data even when everything is working as intended — including by authorized insiders and by the company itself. PII (Personally Identifiable Information — any data that identifies a person, like a name, email, location, or face) is the raw material here. The U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST, the government body that publishes widely-used security standards) gives privacy its own triad:
  • Predictability — people and operators can reliably understand how data is processed. No surprises.
  • Manageability — granular control over data: the ability to correct it, export it, or delete it.
  • Disassociability — process data without unnecessarily linking it to a specific individual (through minimization, collecting less; de-identification; or anonymization).
Privacy's core question: "Should we even be collecting, keeping, or using this — and can the person control it?"

NIST is blunt about the relationship: "security is not privacy and privacy is not security," even though they overlap heavily. Reference frameworks to know by name: the NIST Privacy Framework (a voluntary guide modeled on its famous Cybersecurity Framework; v1.1 was in draft through 2025) and NISTIR 8062 (an introduction to privacy engineering and privacy risk). NIST even runs a dedicated Privacy Engineering Program.

Key takeaway: A perfectly secure system can still be a privacy disaster. A company that encrypts everything, suffers zero breaches, yet quietly harvests users' location data and sells it has a privacy failure, not a security failure. A data breach, by contrast, is usually both at once.

Analogy: Security is the locks, alarms, and walls of a building. Privacy is the policy about who is allowed inside, what they may look at while there, and how long the visitor logbook is kept. You need both — a vault with an open guest list still leaks.
        SECURITY (keep attackers out)      PRIVACY (handle data rightly)
        ----------------------------       -----------------------------
        Confidentiality                    Predictability
        Integrity            overlap  =>   Manageability
        Availability                       Disassociability

  "Can the wrong person get in?"      "Should we collect/keep/use this,
                                        and can the person control it?"

1.2 Why this is a durable 20-year skill (not automated away)

Security is not a fixed checklist you finish. It is an adversarial problem — there is an intelligent opponent on the other side actively trying to defeat whatever you build. That single property makes it fundamentally different from most engineering tasks and explains why demand compounds rather than fades.

The workforce numbers are stark. The 2025 ISC2 Cybersecurity Workforce Study put the global talent gap at roughly 4.8 million unfilled roles; the World Economic Forum estimates the workforce must grow about 87% to meet demand. Tellingly, ISC2 stopped publishing a single headline gap number in 2025 because the bottleneck shifted from raw headcount to skills — practitioners now rate cloud and AI-security expertise as more urgent than warm bodies. The pain is concrete: 88% of organizations reported at least one significant security consequence in the past year due to a skills shortage, and IBM found that organizations with severe staffing shortages pay on average $1.76M more per breach than well-staffed peers.

What about "won't AI just automate this away?" The opposite is true, for two reasons. First, automation removes toil — log triage, basic alert sorting — which frees humans for the parts that resist automation: threat modeling, architecture, incident response, and reasoning about a creative adversary. Second, every new technology (cloud, mobile, IoT, and now AI/LLMs) creates a brand-new attack surface that needs people who understand it. IBM's 2025 report underlines the force-multiplier effect: organizations using AI and automation extensively in defense saved about $1.9M per breach versus those using none.

Best practice: Treat AI as leverage for the security engineer, not a replacement. The framing to internalize: "AI doesn't replace the security engineer; it gives the security engineer leverage — and it creates a whole new category of things that need securing."

1.3 The modern threat landscape: who, and what they want

You can't defend against an attacker you can't picture. Motivation drives technique, so learn the attacker taxonomy.

WhoSkill / ResourcesWhat they wantTypical methods
Script kiddies / opportunistsLowNotoriety, easy access to resellOff-the-shelf exploit kits, automated spray-and-pray
Organized crime / ransomware gangsHigh, business-likeMoneyRansomware-as-a-Service, "double/triple extortion" (encrypt + steal + threaten to leak), fraud
HacktivistsMixedDisruption / attention for a causeDDoS, defacement, doxxing leaks
InsidersTrusted accessMoney, revenge — or honest mistakesMisuse of legitimate access; hard to stop at the perimeter
Nation-states / APTsBest-resourced, patientEspionage, IP, pre-positioning in critical infrastructureStealthy long-term intrusions, zero-days, supply-chain compromise

A few terms: ransomware is malware that encrypts your files and demands payment to unlock them. An APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) is a well-funded, patient attacker — usually a nation-state — that quietly stays inside a network for months. DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) floods a system with traffic to knock it offline. The commonly cited "big four" state actors are China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea (North Korea notably blends espionage with outright theft to fund its regime). Boiled down, attackers want one of three things: MONEY, DATA, or DISRUPTION.

Microsoft's 2025 Digital Defense Report found that extortion and ransomware drive over half of all financially-motivated cyberattacks. And these are not abstract — recent incidents show cyber attacks now stop factories and close hospitals:

Example: Real incidents, 2025–2026:
  • Jaguar Land Rover (Aug 2025) — called the most economically damaging cyber incident in UK history: ~£1.9B cost, production halted ~5 weeks. Cyber became physical.
  • Scattered Spider / Qantas (June 2025) — slick social engineering of help desks exposed up to ~5.7M customers.
  • Microsoft SharePoint "ToolShell" zero-day (July 2025) — Chinese state-linked actors breached U.S. government agencies and global firms via a single unpatched flaw.
  • University of Mississippi Medical Center (Feb 2026) — ransomware closed all 35 clinics statewide and downed the EMR system. Healthcare = life-safety stakes.
  • Poland's energy grid (Dec 2025, ~30 OT sites, Russia-linked) and Romania's national water authority (Dec 2025) — critical infrastructure under direct fire.
Macro picture: ransomware against critical infrastructure rose ~34% year-over-year; ~44% of breaches involved ransomware; ~30% traced to supply-chain/third-party failures; estimated global cybercrime cost ~$10.5 trillion in 2025.

1.4 How AI expands the attack surface (the defender's new problem)

Shipping an AI feature is not like shipping a normal feature — it introduces vulnerability classes that simply did not exist before. The reference to anchor on is the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (OWASP is a respected nonprofit that publishes lists of the most critical software risks; LLM = Large Language Model, the technology behind chatbots like Claude or ChatGPT). The 2025 edition headlines:

LLM01 — Prompt injection (the #1 risk)
Tricking the model into ignoring its real instructions. Direct injection: a user types malicious instructions. Indirect injection: the model reads poisoned external content — a webpage, email, PDF, or document — that secretly hijacks it. If the model can use tools or act as an agent, this becomes arbitrary actions in connected systems. The 2026 frontier includes agentic "skill-file" injection and encoding tricks.
Sensitive-data exposure & exfiltration
Models can leak training data, secrets, or other users' data; agents can be tricked into emailing or exporting data they should never touch.
New 2025 categories
System Prompt Leakage (the model reveals its hidden instructions) and Vector & Embedding Weaknesses (poisoning the data store behind RAG systems — RAG = Retrieval-Augmented Generation, where a model looks things up before answering).
AI/ML supply-chain & model risk
Poisoned model weights and malicious models on public hubs. In 2025 researchers flagged that a large share of top-downloaded Hugging Face models had been compromised at some point; "model namespace reuse" lets attackers re-register abandoned model names to ship malicious replacements; malicious LoRA adapters are a small, easy-to-spread new vector.
Common mistake: Bolting an LLM onto a product and assuming traditional app-security controls cover it. IBM 2025 found 13% of organizations reported breaches of AI models or apps — and 97% of those lacked proper AI access controls. Meanwhile 63% of breached organizations had no AI governance policy at all. "Shadow AI" (employees using ungoverned AI tools) caused breaches at 1 in 5 organizations and added ~$670,000 to the average breach cost.

1.5 How attackers use AI (offense gets cheaper and better)

AI lowers the skill floor — script kiddies now punch far above their weight — and raises the ceiling, letting pros scale and personalize attacks. Concrete data points:

  • AI-written phishing (fraudulent messages that trick you into revealing credentials or clicking malware) reaches ~54% click-through versus ~12% for traditional phishing, with full campaigns built in ~5 minutes at ~95% lower cost. By early 2025, roughly 32% of phishing emails showed signs of LLM authorship.
  • Criminal LLMs like WormGPT (~$60/mo) and FraudGPT (~$200/mo) are sold like SaaS for phishing, malware, and obfuscation.
  • Deepfakes & voice cloning grew ~680% year-over-year in fraud. A single multi-channel deepfake video-call scam (the Hong Kong "CFO" fraud) caused ~$25M in losses; an earlier cloned-voice scam tricked a UK executive into wiring $243,000.
  • State actors have been caught misusing public LLMs — North Korean and Iranian groups generating phishing lures and social-engineering content (disclosed via OpenAI's threat-intelligence disruptions).
Key takeaway: AI security is an arms race — and an arms race is exactly the condition under which skilled human defenders become more valuable, not less.

1.6 The tightening regulatory wave

Regulation turns security and privacy from "nice to have" into legal and financial liability — and someone has to engineer the controls those laws require (data deletion, consent capture, access logging, model documentation). That someone is you.

  • Privacy law is now global. Around 144 countries (covering ~79% of the world's population) have data-protection laws in force (IAPP). The EU's GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is the template the world copies — the so-called "Brussels effect." GDPR penalties reach 4% of global annual turnover or €20M, whichever is higher.
  • The U.S. is a state-by-state patchwork of 20+ comprehensive privacy laws (California's CCPA/CPRA is the flagship, plus Virginia, Colorado, Texas, Oregon, and more). The compliance complexity itself drives demand for engineers who can implement controls once and satisfy many laws.
  • The EU AI Act — the world's first comprehensive AI law — is phasing in. Prohibited-AI bans and AI-literacy duties went live Feb 2, 2025; general-purpose AI model obligations from Aug 2, 2025; enforcement powers (including fines) from Aug 2, 2026. A May 2026 "Digital Omnibus" provisional deal defers some high-risk obligations to Dec 2, 2027. Fines reach €35M or 7% of global turnover — higher than GDPR.

1.7 What breaches actually cost

The must-cite source is the IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2025:

Metric2025 figureWhat it tells you
Global average breach cost$4.44M (down 9%)First decline in 5 years — but only because AI helped defenders contain faster
United States average$10.22M (record, up 9%)Costs are diverging by region; the US got worse
AI/automation defender savings~$1.9M saved per breachTooling pays for itself many times over
Most common causePhishing (~16%, ~$4.8M avg)Humans remain the entry point
Shadow-AI premium+$670KUngoverned AI is expensive
Staffing-shortage premium+$1.76MSkilled people directly lower cost

The nuance matters: the global average dropped only because AI sped up containment (mean time to identify and contain fell to 241 days, a nine-year low) — while US, AI-related, and understaffed breaches all got worse. The lesson is not "we're winning." It's that the gap between prepared and unprepared organizations is widening fast.

1.8 Misconceptions to unlearn now

  • "Security is the security team's job." No — engineers build the vulnerabilities, so engineers must build the defenses. This is "shift-left": handle security early, in design and code, not bolted on at the end.
  • "Privacy equals security." No — distinct disciplines with distinct triads (§1.1).
  • "AI will automate security away." Opposite: it expands the attack surface and multiplies defender leverage.
  • "We're too small to be a target." Most attacks are automated and opportunistic; small businesses and supply-chain vendors are prime targets (~30% of breaches are supply-chain).
  • "Compliant means secure." Compliance is a floor, not a ceiling. You can be fully compliant and still breached.

Common mistakes (quick list)

  • Treating privacy as a subset of security (or vice versa).
  • Adding an LLM feature without new AI-specific threat modeling.
  • Assuming automation reduces the need for security expertise.
  • Believing passing an audit means you are actually safe.
  • Thinking your organization is too small or too boring to be attacked.

Best practices (quick list)

  • Hold security and privacy as two distinct goals, each with its own checklist (CIA triad and the NIST privacy triad).
  • Shift left — make security and privacy a design-time concern owned by every engineer.
  • Use AI as a force multiplier for defense, and threat-model any AI feature you ship.
  • Build for the strictest regulation you touch; one well-engineered control can satisfy many laws.
  • Measure your readiness gap — the cost of being unprepared is now millions, and rising.

1.9 What the rest of this guide builds

Now that you know why this matters — durable demand, escalating and AI-armed adversaries, a global regulatory wave, and real money on the line — the rest of the guide builds the how: threat modeling (thinking like an attacker on purpose), the CIA and privacy triads applied in practice, secure-design principles such as least privilege and defense in depth, the OWASP risk catalogs, AI/LLM-specific defenses, and privacy-by-design. Approach it as a craft you'll practice for the next 20 years — because the adversary never stops innovating, and that is precisely what makes it worth mastering.

Key takeaway: Security engineering keeps attackers out (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability); privacy engineering governs how you handle people's data even when nothing is broken (Predictability, Manageability, Disassociability). The field is growing, durably human, and AI-amplified on both offense and defense. With a ~4.8M-role gap, multi-million-dollar breaches, and a worldwide wave of GDPR-style and AI laws turning these concerns into hard liability, engineers who can actually build the controls don't get automated away — they become the most valuable people in the room.

sections.title: Core Security Foundations
sections.html:
2. Core Security Foundations

Before you learn any specific attack or defense, you need the mental scaffolding that every security engineer reasons from. This section gives you five building blocks, in order: what we protect (the CIA triad), how we control who gets in (AAA), how we build systems safely (design principles), how we talk precisely about danger (vulnerability, threat, risk, exploit), and how we think (the security mindset, trust boundaries, attack surface, and the shared responsibility model). Master these and almost everything else in security becomes a variation on a theme you already understand.

2.1 The CIA Triad — what "secure" actually means

The CIA triad (nothing to do with the spy agency) is the canonical model of the three properties that security exists to preserve. Use it as a checklist: when you read about any attack, ask "which leg does this hit?"

Confidentiality
Only authorized parties can read the data. Mechanisms: encryption (scrambling data so only key-holders can read it) at rest and in transit, access control, multi-factor authentication (MFA), and data classification. A violation is a data breach — information disclosed to people who shouldn't see it.
Integrity
Data is accurate, complete, and changed only by authorized actions. Mechanisms: hashing (a fingerprint that changes if even one byte changes), digital signatures, checksums, version control, input validation, and write permissions. A violation is tampering, fraud, or corruption.
Availability
Authorized users can reach the system and data when they need it. Mechanisms: redundancy, backups, DDoS protection, failover, and capacity planning. A violation is an outage, ransomware lockout, or denial-of-service.
Example: The 2024 Change Healthcare breach is a textbook confidentiality failure. Attackers entered through a Citrix remote-access portal that had no MFA, exfiltrated roughly 4 TB of data, then encrypted the systems. In January 2025 UnitedHealth confirmed about 190 million Americans' records were exposed — the largest U.S. healthcare breach ever, costing over $2.45 billion. The single missing confidentiality control (MFA) opened the door.
Example: Ransomware famously hits two legs at once. The 2023 MGM Resorts attack began with one vishing (voice phishing) call to the IT help desk; within hours slot machines, digital room keys, reservations, and ATMs went dark across 30+ properties — an availability disaster. Modern "double extortion" gangs also leak the stolen data, so they break confidentiality too. The 2024 CDK Global attack left car dealerships unable to write contracts or book service for weeks — pure availability loss.
Analogy: Think of a library. Confidentiality = only members can read the restricted archive. Integrity = nobody secretly rewrites the books. Availability = the doors are open when you arrive.

The three legs are in tension. Perfect confidentiality — lock everything in a vault with no network — destroys availability. Security engineering is the art of balancing them according to how valuable the data is. Some models extend CIA with Authenticity, Non-repudiation, and Accountability; the "Parkerian Hexad" adds Possession and Utility. Know they exist, but keep CIA central.

2.2 AAA — how access is controlled

AAA is a three-step access framework that originated in networking (RADIUS and TACACS+ protocols) and is now used everywhere. The order matters.

StepQuestionPlain meaning2025 OWASP link
Authentication (AuthN)"Who are you?"Prove identity. Factors: something you know (password), have (phone/token), or are (fingerprint). MFA = 2+ factors.A07 Authentication Failures
Authorization (AuthZ)"What may you do?"Decided after AuthN. Models: RBAC (role-based), ABAC (attribute-based).A01 Broken Access Control
Accounting (Auditing)"What did you do?"Log every action for forensics, billing, compliance, and non-repudiation.A09 Logging & Alerting Failures
Common mistake: Confusing AuthN with AuthZ. AuthN is identity ("you are Alice"); AuthZ is permission ("Alice may view order #42 but not #43"). The web's #1 risk — Broken Access Control — is an AuthZ failure. Classic case: an IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference), where changing /order?id=42 to id=43 shows you someone else's order because the server checked who you are but never whether this record is yours.

2.3 Core design principles

Most of these come from Saltzer & Schroeder's 1975 paper "The Protection of Information in Computer Systems" — eight principles still taught 50 years later. OWASP's Developer Guide modernizes them. Treat them not as checkboxes but as trade-off lenses you apply while designing.

  1. Defense in depth — layer multiple independent controls so one failure isn't fatal. A modern addition, not in the original eight.
  2. Least privilege — give every user, process, and service the minimum rights to do its job, no more. This limits the "blast radius" when something is compromised, and is core to Zero Trust.
  3. Fail securely / fail-safe defaults — when something errors, default to deny, not allow. Grant access on explicit permission, never by accidental fall-through.
  4. Separation of duties (SoD) — no single person has enough power to abuse the system alone. The requester of a payment must not also be its approver.
  5. Complete mediation — every access to every object is checked for authority every time. Never cache a "yes" and trust it forever.
  6. Secure defaults — ship locked down; users opt into risk, not out of safety. Default passwords and open storage buckets are anti-patterns.
  7. Economy of mechanism (KISS) — keep the design as small and simple as possible. Complexity hides bugs and resists auditing.
  8. Open design / no security through obscurity — security must not depend on the design being secret (Kerckhoffs's Principle, 1883: a system should stay secure even if everything but the key is public).
  9. Psychological acceptability — if security is too painful, users bypass it. The secure path must be the easy path. Usability is a security property.
  10. Least common mechanism — minimize resources and state shared between users, to limit cross-contamination.
Analogy: A castle shows defense in depth — moat, wall, gate, guards. If MGM had layered help-desk identity verification beyond a single phone call, one social-engineering attempt could not have collapsed everything.
Common mistake: "Fail open" by accident. A locked door that unlocks when power fails is failing insecurely. In code, an auth check that crashes and lets the request through is the same bug — exactly OWASP 2025's new A10 "Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions."
Common mistake: Believing that hiding your source code or algorithm makes you secure. DVD CSS encryption relied on secrecy and was broken the moment it was reverse-engineered. Obscurity can be a thin extra layer, never the control.

OWASP added A06:2025 "Insecure Design" precisely because so many flaws are architectural, not coding bugs — you cannot patch your way out of a bad design.

2.4 The vocabulary you must never conflate

Asset
Anything of value worth protecting — data, systems, money, people, reputation, intellectual property. You cannot secure what you have not inventoried.
Vulnerability
A weakness that could be exploited — unpatched software, weak password, missing MFA, misconfiguration. Public ones are catalogued as CVEs; OWASP's 2025 analysis drew on 175,000+ CVE records.
Threat
A potential event or actor that could exploit a vulnerability to cause harm — a ransomware gang, a flood, a careless insider. Threats exist whether or not you are vulnerable.
Exploit
The actual technique or code that takes advantage of a specific vulnerability. A zero-day is an exploit for a vulnerability that has no patch yet.
Risk
Risk = Likelihood × Impact. This is what executives actually manage. Risk is reducible but rarely zero; leftover (residual) risk is accepted, transferred (insurance), or avoided.
Analogy: A cracked window = vulnerability. A burglar in the neighborhood = threat. The burglar's crowbar method = exploit. "How likely + how bad if they break in" = risk. The jewelry inside = asset.

Threat actors are categorized by motivation and skill: cybercriminals (financial, ransomware-as-a-service), nation-states / APTs (espionage and sabotage, long undetected dwell times), hacktivists (ideological), insiders (malicious or merely negligent — the biggest blind spot), and script kiddies (low skill, public tools). Note the human factor: MGM and Change Healthcare both began with people-and-process gaps, not exotic zero-days.

2.5 How to think: the security mindset

Bruce Schneier described the security mindset (2008): security professionals instinctively ask "how can this be made to fail or be abused?" — the opposite of normal engineering, which asks "how do I make this work?" You think like an attacker, hunt for the unintended path, and distrust assumptions. It is learnable, not innate.

Attack surface
The sum of all points where an attacker could try to get in or pull data out — every input, API, open port, dependency, employee, and third party. The goal is to minimize it. Your software supply chain is now a huge slice, which is why OWASP added A03:2025 "Software Supply Chain Failures."
Trust boundary
A line where the level of trust or control changes — user→app, app→database, your code→third-party API, internet→internal network. Data crossing a trust boundary must be validated and authenticated. Every trust boundary is also attack surface. Zero Trust means "never trust, always verify" — assume the boundary is already breached.
        TRUST BOUNDARY        TRUST BOUNDARY       TRUST BOUNDARY
            |                     |                    |
 Internet --|-->  Your App  ------|-->  Database  ------|--> 3rd-party API
 (untrusted)|   (validate &       |   (authZ each      |   (verify certs,
            |    sanitize all     |    query, least    |    scope tokens,
            |    input here)      |    privilege)      |    least trust)
 attacker probes EVERY arrow = the attack surface; validate at EVERY '|'

2.6 The shared responsibility model (cloud)

In the cloud, security is split between provider and customer. The mantra: the provider secures "of the cloud" (hardware, hypervisor, datacenter, managed-service internals); the customer secures "in the cloud" (their data, identity and access configuration, OS patching, app code, network rules). The split shifts by service model.

ModelProvider securesCustomer always secures
IaaS (raw servers)Hardware, virtualizationOS & up: patching, app, data, access, network
PaaS (managed platform)OS, runtimeApp code, data, identity/access config
SaaS (finished app)Almost everythingData classification, identity governance, access control
Common mistake: Assuming the cloud provider secures your data. Gartner projected through 2025 that about 99% of cloud security failures would be the customer's fault — almost always misconfiguration. That is why "Security Misconfiguration" jumped to OWASP A02:2025. The cloud doesn't make you secure; it redraws the trust boundaries you own.

2.7 Current framework anchors (2025–2026)

  • OWASP Top 10:2025 — announced Nov 2025, finalized Jan 2026. Order: A01 Broken Access Control (still #1), A02 Security Misconfiguration, A03 Software Supply Chain Failures (new), A04 Cryptographic Failures, A05 Injection, A06 Insecure Design, A07 Authentication Failures, A08 Software/Data Integrity Failures, A09 Logging & Alerting Failures, A10 Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions (new). SSRF folded into A01.
  • NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0 — released Feb 2024, first major update since 2014. Added a sixth, central function, Govern, wrapping the original five (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover). Now meant for all organizations, not just critical infrastructure.
  • IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2025 — global average $4.44M (down ~9% year-on-year, credited to faster AI-assisted detection); the U.S. hit a record $10.22M. Your "why it matters" number.
  • EU AI Act — prohibited-practice and AI-literacy rules live since Feb 2, 2025; general-purpose AI obligations and the penalty regime (fines up to €35M or 7% of global turnover) since Aug 2, 2025; most high-risk rules apply Aug 2026. Security now intersects AI governance and compliance.

Common mistakes

  • Confusing authentication (who you are) with authorization (what you may do).
  • Thinking encryption alone equals "secure" — it ignores integrity and availability.
  • Believing hidden code is secure (obscurity is not a control).
  • Using "threat" and "risk" as synonyms.
  • Assuming the cloud provider protects your data.
  • Letting error handlers "fail open" by accident.
  • Bolting security on at the end instead of designing it in.

Best practices

  • For every feature, ask which CIA leg it affects and which design principle it tests.
  • Default to deny; require explicit permission for access.
  • Apply least privilege to users, services, and machine accounts alike.
  • Validate and authenticate all data crossing a trust boundary.
  • Minimize attack surface — fewer ports, features, and dependencies.
  • Make the secure path the easy path so people don't route around it.
  • Log enough to investigate later; you can't detect what you didn't record.
Key takeaway: Security reasoning rests on five pillars — what we protect (CIA: confidentiality, integrity, availability), how we control access (AAA: authentication, authorization, accounting), how we build (design principles led by least privilege, defense in depth, fail-secure, and complete mediation), how we describe danger (asset → vulnerability → threat → exploit → risk = likelihood × impact), and how we think (the attacker's mindset, mapping trust boundaries and attack surface, and owning your share of cloud responsibility). The 2025–26 frameworks — OWASP Top 10:2025 and NIST CSF 2.0's new Govern function — are simply these foundations written down at scale.

sections.title: Cryptography Made Simple
sections.html:
3. Cryptography Made Simple

Cryptography sounds like math, but as a working engineer you almost never write the math. Your job is to pick the right tool for the right goal and not misuse it. So forget the equations and learn crypto by its three purposes. Cryptography (from Greek "hidden writing") is the science of protecting information so that only the people who should read or trust it can. Everything below serves one of three goals:

Confidentiality
Only the intended reader can see the content. (Tool: encryption.)
Integrity
The content was not changed, even by one bit. (Tool: hashing.)
Authenticity
It really came from who it claims to be from. (Tool: digital signatures.)
Key takeaway: When you face a crypto decision, ask "which of the three goals do I need?" first. Most real-world breaches happen because someone reached for the wrong tool (e.g., encrypting passwords instead of hashing them), not because the algorithm was weak.

3.1 Encoding vs Hashing vs Encryption — the #1 confusion

These three words get mixed up constantly, and that confusion has caused real breaches. They are completely different things.

Encoding
A reversible reformatting with no secret involved. Examples: Base64, URL-encoding, ASCII. Purpose is transport/format, never security. Anyone can decode it. Analogy: translating English into Morse code — no secret, anyone with the chart reads it.
Hashing
A one-way fingerprint. The same input always produces the same fixed-length output; you cannot reverse it back to the original. A hash function like SHA-256 turns any input into 256 bits (64 hex characters). A tiny change in input flips most of the output bits — this is the avalanche effect. Analogy: blending fruit into a smoothie — you cannot un-blend it back into the original fruit.
Encryption
A reversible scramble that needs a key. Purpose is confidentiality. Without the key, the output (called ciphertext) is useless gibberish; with the key, you get the exact original back. Analogy: a locked box — the key opens it, nobody else can.
Common mistake: Treating Base64 as "encryption." Base64 is encoding — it hides nothing. Putting a Base64 string in a token or config gives zero security; anyone decodes it in one line.
Example: The 2013 Adobe breach exposed ~38 million users. Adobe encrypted passwords with reversible 3DES instead of hashing them, and used the same key in the broken ECB mode for everyone. Once the pattern leaked, identical passwords showed identical ciphertext and millions of passwords became recoverable. Passwords must be hashed (one-way), never encrypted (reversible).

3.2 Symmetric Encryption (AES) — one shared key

Symmetric encryption uses one secret key for both encrypting and decrypting. It is fast, so it is what actually protects bulk data (files, disks, the body of a web request). The standard is AES (Advanced Encryption Standard), adopted in 2001, with 128-, 192-, or 256-bit keys. AES-256 is the everyday workhorse.

But how you run AES matters enormously — this is the mode of operation. Never use ECB (Electronic Codebook) mode: it encrypts each block independently, so identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext, leaking the data's patterns. The famous "ECB Penguin" demo shows an image still clearly recognizable after ECB "encryption." Instead use authenticated modes — AES-256-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 — which give confidentiality and integrity together (they detect tampering).

Common mistake: Reusing the same nonce/IV (a "number used once" that makes each encryption unique) with GCM. Nonce reuse is catastrophic — it can leak the key stream and even the authentication key. Generate a fresh random nonce every time.

Symmetric crypto has one big problem: two strangers on an open network must somehow agree on the same secret key without an eavesdropper learning it. That problem is solved by public-key crypto and key exchange.

3.3 Asymmetric / Public-Key Crypto (RSA, ECC) and Key Exchange

Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair: a public key (shareable with anyone) and a private key (kept secret). Anyone can encrypt a message to you using your public key, but only your private key can decrypt it.

Analogy: Your public key is an open padlock you mail to people. They snap it shut on a box and send it back. Only you hold the key that opens that padlock. They never needed your key to lock it.

RSA (1977) relies on the difficulty of factoring huge numbers and needs big keys (2048 or 4096 bits) — secure but slow. ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography) gives the same security with far smaller keys (a 256-bit ECC key ≈ a 3072-bit RSA key), so it is faster and now dominates TLS.

Asymmetric crypto is slow, so in practice it is used only to set up a shared symmetric key, then fast AES does the bulk work. This is hybrid encryption, and it is how essentially all secure connections work.

Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange (1976) lets two parties derive the same shared secret over a public channel without ever sending it.

Analogy (mixing paint): Both sides start with a public color. Each privately adds a secret color, then they swap mixtures. Each adds their private color again. Both end with the identical final mix — but an eavesdropper who saw the swapped mixtures cannot un-mix them to find the secret.

Modern TLS uses ECDHE (Elliptic-Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral). "Ephemeral" means a fresh throwaway key for every session. This gives forward secrecy: even if the server's long-term private key is stolen later, past recorded sessions stay safe because each used a random, discarded key. ECDHE is the default in TLS 1.3.

            ENCRYPTION (private decrypts)        SIGNATURE (private signs)
 Sender --[encrypt w/ recipient PUBLIC key]-->   Signer --[sign hash w/ OWN PRIVATE key]-->
 Recipient --[decrypt w/ OWN PRIVATE key]        Anyone --[verify w/ signer PUBLIC key]
 Goal: CONFIDENTIALITY                           Goal: AUTHENTICITY + INTEGRITY

3.4 Hashing for Integrity vs Hashing for Passwords

General-purpose hashing uses SHA-256 (part of the SHA-2 family; SHA-3 exists as an alternate design). It is used for file-download verification, digital signatures, blockchain, and deduplication. Crucially, SHA-256 is fast by design — perfect for integrity, but terrible for passwords, because an attacker with a GPU can try billions of guesses per second.

For passwords you want slowness. Use a purpose-built password KDF (Key Derivation Function) — an intentionally slow, resource-hungry hash:

AlgorithmUse it?Notes / OWASP 2024 settings
Argon2idFirst choiceMemory-hard (resists GPU/ASIC). m=19–64 MiB, t=2–3, p=1 (~250–400 ms/hash). NIST/OWASP/RFC 9106 recommended.
scryptGood fallbackMemory-hard. Min cost 2^17, block 8, parallelism 1.
bcryptOK legacyWork factor 12+. Caveat: silently truncates passwords past 72 bytes.
PBKDF2Only if forcedUse only for FIPS compliance; needs very high iteration counts.
MD5 / SHA-1 / plain SHA-256NEVER for passwordsToo fast; MD5/SHA-1 are cryptographically broken (collisions).
Salt
A unique random value per password, stored next to the hash. It defeats rainbow tables (precomputed hash lookup tables) and ensures two users with the same password get different hashes. Non-negotiable — modern KDFs add it automatically.
Pepper
A single secret value shared across all passwords, stored separately from the database (in a KMS/HSM or app config). Even if the entire database leaks, hashes cannot be cracked without the pepper.
Example: The 2012 LinkedIn breach used unsalted SHA-1. About 6.5M hashes leaked (later ~117M+ accounts surfaced) and were cracked en masse with rainbow tables, leading to a class-action settlement. A salted Argon2id store would have made mass cracking infeasible.

3.5 Digital Signatures — proving authenticity and integrity

A digital signature proves a message truly came from a sender and was not altered (it does not hide the content). The signer hashes the message, then encrypts that hash with their private key — that encrypted hash is the signature. Anyone uses the signer's public key to decrypt the signature and compares the result to a fresh hash of the message. If they match, it is authentic and unaltered. Bonus: non-repudiation — the signer cannot later deny it. Notice this is encryption run backwards: private key signs, public key verifies. Signatures power TLS certificates, code signing, and software updates.

Analogy: A wax seal pressed with a unique signet ring. Anyone can see the seal and recognize the ring (public), but only the ring's owner could have made it (private), and a broken seal proves tampering.

3.6 TLS / HTTPS Handshake, Certificates, and Let's Encrypt

TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the protocol behind the padlock in https://. It combines everything above. A simplified TLS 1.3 handshake:

 Browser                                   Server
   | --- ClientHello: ciphers + DH share ---> |
   | <-- ServerHello: chosen cipher,          |
   |     server DH share, CERTIFICATE ------- |
   | --- both compute shared key via ECDHE -- |
   | --- verify cert chains to trusted CA --- |
   | === encrypted traffic (AES/ChaCha20) === |
